items.q: Why are passwords stored as hashes rather than plaintext?
items.correct: Hashes are one-way - even an attacker who steals them cannot easily recover passwords.
items.misconception: Hashes are encryption that can be decrypted with the right key.
items.random_wrong: Hashes compress the data to save space.
items.why: Students confuse hashing with encryption. Hashes have NO reverse function - you can verify a password by re-hashing, but cannot decrypt the stored hash to get the original.

items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.

items.q: What is hexadecimal A in denary?
items.why: Students forget hex uses A-F to represent 10-15 because we need 16 distinct symbols. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

items.q: Which technique involves breaking a problem into smaller sub-problems?
items.why: Correct answer: Decomposition. “Abstraction” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the purpose of a trace table?
items.correct: To track variable values during execution
items.why: It's To track variable values during execution. “To compile code” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many distinct shapes are commonly used in basic flowcharts (start/end, process, decision, input/output)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 shapes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
